P0011.shortDescription: Camshaft Position A - Timing Over-Advanced or System Performance (Bank 1)
P0011.detailedDescription: The Engine Control Module detected that the intake camshaft on Bank 1 is more advanced than the commanded position, or the variable valve timing system is not responding correctly to commands.
commonCauses: Low or dirty engine oil restricting VVT actuator
Failed VVT (variable valve timing) solenoid on Bank 1
Clogged VVT oil control valve screen
Wiring fault to the VVT solenoid

P0014.shortDescription: Camshaft Position B - Timing Over-Advanced or System Performance (Bank 1)
P0014.detailedDescription: The exhaust camshaft on Bank 1 is over-advanced relative to the commanded position, indicating a VVT control problem on the exhaust cam.

P0016.shortDescription: Crankshaft Position - Camshaft Position Correlation (Bank 1 Sensor A)
P0016.detailedDescription: The PCM has detected the crankshaft and intake camshaft position sensors are not in correlation. This often indicates a timing issue.
commonCauses: Bad camshaft or crankshaft position sensor
Low oil pressure to VVT system
Wiring or connector issue at cam/crank sensors

P0017.shortDescription: Crankshaft Position - Camshaft Position Correlation (Bank 1 Sensor B)
P0017.detailedDescription: Crank-to-exhaust-cam correlation on Bank 1 is out of spec, suggesting a timing or VVT system issue on the exhaust side.

P0101.detailedDescription: The MAF sensor is providing a signal that is outside the expected range relative to other engine inputs (RPM, throttle position, etc.). The sensor is reading but the value is implausible.
commonCauses: Dirty or contaminated MAF sensor element
Unmetered air leak after the MAF (intake boot, vacuum line)

P0102.shortDescription: Mass Air Flow Sensor Circuit Low Input
P0102.detailedDescription: The MAF sensor signal voltage is below the expected minimum, suggesting an open circuit or sensor failure.
commonCauses: Wiring open or shorted to ground

P0113.shortDescription: Intake Air Temperature Sensor 1 Circuit High
P0113.detailedDescription: IAT sensor circuit voltage is higher than expected, typically indicating an open circuit or a disconnected sensor.
commonCauses: Disconnected or corroded IAT sensor connector
Open circuit in IAT signal wire
Failed IAT sensor (often integrated into MAF)

P0118.shortDescription: Engine Coolant Temperature Sensor Circuit High
P0118.detailedDescription: The ECT sensor voltage is above the normal range, indicating an open circuit or extreme cold reading.

P0128.detailedDescription: Engine coolant temperature did not reach the expected operating temperature within the expected time. Usually a stuck-open thermostat.

P0131.shortDescription: O2 Sensor Circuit Low Voltage (Bank 1, Sensor 1)
P0131.detailedDescription: Upstream O2 sensor on Bank 1 is reporting voltage below the expected range, suggesting a lean condition or sensor fault.
commonCauses: Vacuum leak causing actual lean condition
Short to ground in sensor wiring
Fuel delivery problem (low pressure, weak pump)

P0132.shortDescription: O2 Sensor Circuit High Voltage (Bank 1, Sensor 1)
P0132.detailedDescription: Bank 1 upstream O2 sensor voltage is higher than expected, suggesting a rich condition or signal short to voltage.
commonCauses: Short to voltage in sensor signal wire

P0135.shortDescription: O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1, Sensor 1)
P0135.detailedDescription: The heater circuit inside the upstream O2 sensor on Bank 1 is not drawing the expected current. Usually the heater element itself has failed.

P0136.detailedDescription: Downstream O2 sensor on Bank 1 is reporting an out-of-range signal.
commonCauses: Exhaust leak between catalyst and sensor
Contaminated sensor from coolant or oil burning

P0141.shortDescription: O2 Sensor Heater Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1, Sensor 2)
P0141.detailedDescription: The heater circuit inside the downstream O2 sensor on Bank 1 has failed or is out of range.
commonCauses: Blown heater fuse (shared with other heaters)

P0171.detailedDescription: Long term fuel trims on Bank 1 are above the threshold to compensate for an apparent lean condition — the PCM is commanding more fuel than the calibrated norm to keep the air/fuel ratio at stoichiometry.
commonCauses: Vacuum/intake leak (most common — check intake boot, PCV hose, brake booster)
Weak fuel pump / low fuel pressure
Leaking fuel injector or stuck-open EVAP purge
Exhaust leak ahead of the upstream O2 sensor

P0172.detailedDescription: Bank 1 fuel trims are subtracting fuel to compensate for an apparent rich condition.
commonCauses: Saturated EVAP charcoal canister or stuck-open purge
Coolant temp sensor reading colder than actual

P0174.detailedDescription: Same as P0171 but on Bank 2. If both P0171 and P0174 are present, the issue is on the air side common to both banks (MAF, vacuum leak in plenum, etc.).

P0175.detailedDescription: Bank 2 fuel trims are subtracting fuel to compensate for a rich condition.
commonCauses: Faulty MAF or coolant temp sensor

P0299.detailedDescription: The forced induction system is not producing the commanded boost pressure.
commonCauses: Boost leak in charge pipes or intercooler

P0300.detailedDescription: The PCM has detected misfires occurring randomly or across multiple cylinders rather than concentrated on a single cylinder. The PCM monitors crankshaft acceleration between firing events to detect this.
commonCauses: Worn or fouled spark plugs (most common — start here)
Low fuel pressure or failing fuel pump

P0316.detailedDescription: A misfire was detected during the first 1000 engine revolutions after startup. Usually points to a fault that clears once the engine warms up.
commonCauses: Stuck-open injector that drains pressure overnight

P0325.detailedDescription: The Bank 1 knock sensor signal is missing or out of range. The PCM uses the knock sensor to detect detonation and pull ignition timing if needed.
commonCauses: Bad connector at the sensor (often under the intake manifold)

P0335.shortDescription: Crankshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Malfunction
P0335.detailedDescription: No signal or out-of-range signal from the crankshaft position sensor. The engine may not start or may stall intermittently.
commonCauses: Wiring or connector damage at the sensor
Metal debris stuck to the sensor

P0340.shortDescription: Camshaft Position Sensor A Circuit Malfunction (Bank 1)
P0340.detailedDescription: Missing or implausible signal from the Bank 1 intake camshaft position sensor.
commonCauses: Damaged reluctor wheel on the cam
Timing chain stretched (signal out of phase)

P0420.detailedDescription: The PCM compared upstream and downstream O2 sensor signals on Bank 1 and determined the catalytic converter is not storing/releasing enough oxygen — usually indicating catalyst degradation.
commonCauses: Engine misfire damaging the cat (look for misfire codes first)
Engine running rich/lean (oil or coolant burning into the cat)
Failed downstream O2 sensor reporting incorrectly
Exhaust leak between cat and downstream sensor

P0430.detailedDescription: Same as P0420 but on Bank 2.
commonCauses: Misfire on Bank 2 damaging the cat

P0440.detailedDescription: Generic EVAP system fault detected by the PCM. The EVAP system captures fuel vapors from the tank and purges them through the engine.
commonCauses: Failed purge valve or vent valve

P0442.shortDescription: Evaporative Emission Control System Small Leak Detected
P0442.detailedDescription: A small leak (typically 0.040 inch or 1mm equivalent) was detected in the EVAP system during the leak test.
commonCauses: Loose or worn fuel cap seal (check first — free fix)

P0446.shortDescription: Evaporative Emission Control System Vent Control Circuit Malfunction
P0446.detailedDescription: The EVAP vent valve circuit is reporting an electrical fault or the valve is not responding to commands.
commonCauses: Wiring or connector damage at the vent valve (often under the vehicle near the canister)
Blocked vent valve (debris, ice, spider webs)

P0455.shortDescription: Evaporative Emission Control System Large Leak Detected
P0455.detailedDescription: A large leak was detected in the EVAP system during the leak test.
commonCauses: Loose or missing fuel cap (very common)

P0456.shortDescription: Evaporative Emission Control System Very Small Leak Detected
P0456.detailedDescription: A very small leak (typically 0.020 inch) was detected. Hardest of the EVAP leak codes to diagnose.
commonCauses: Small leak at a fitting or O-ring

P0500.detailedDescription: The PCM is not receiving a valid vehicle speed signal.
commonCauses: ABS module not transmitting speed (newer vehicles)

P0506.shortDescription: Idle Air Control System RPM Lower Than Expected
P0506.detailedDescription: Idle RPM is below the commanded value despite the IAC system being open.
commonCauses: Dirty or failed idle air control valve

P0507.shortDescription: Idle Air Control System RPM Higher Than Expected
P0507.detailedDescription: Idle RPM is above the commanded value, suggesting unmetered air entering the engine.
commonCauses: Stuck-open IAC valve or throttle body
Failed throttle body relearn after cleaning

P0562.detailedDescription: Battery/charging system voltage measured at the PCM is below the normal operating range.

P0606.detailedDescription: The PCM detected an internal processor fault. This is usually a hardware-level issue inside the PCM itself.
commonCauses: Failed PCM (replacement and reprogramming often required)
Severe voltage spike damaged the PCM

P0700.detailedDescription: Generic flag set by the PCM indicating the Transmission Control Module has reported one or more faults. You must scan the TCM for the specific sub-codes.
commonCauses: Scan the TCM for additional codes — P0700 alone tells you to look deeper
Common sub-codes: shift solenoid faults (P0750+), torque converter clutch (P0740+), input/output speed sensors (P0715-P0723)

P0720.detailedDescription: The transmission output speed sensor is not providing a valid signal.
commonCauses: Metal debris on the sensor (failing transmission internals)

P0741.shortDescription: Torque Converter Clutch Circuit Performance / Stuck Off
P0741.detailedDescription: The torque converter clutch is not engaging when commanded, or slip exceeds the expected threshold.
commonCauses: Worn TCC friction material inside the converter

P0750.detailedDescription: Electrical fault detected on shift solenoid A circuit.

B1318.detailedDescription: A body control module detected battery voltage below operating threshold during key-on.

B1676.shortDescription: Battery Pack Voltage Out of Range
B1676.detailedDescription: Vehicle battery voltage is outside the acceptable operating range as seen by the body system.

C0035.shortDescription: Left Front Wheel Speed Sensor Circuit
C0035.detailedDescription: The ABS module is not receiving a valid signal from the left front wheel speed sensor.
commonCauses: Damaged tone ring on the hub
Metal debris stuck to the sensor
Wiring damage in the harness (chafed near the strut)

C0040.shortDescription: Right Front Wheel Speed Sensor Circuit
C0040.detailedDescription: ABS module not receiving valid signal from the right front wheel speed sensor.

U0073.shortDescription: Control Module Communication Bus A Off
U0073.detailedDescription: A control module has stopped communicating on the high-speed CAN bus.
commonCauses: Failed module connected to the bus
CAN bus wiring fault (short to ground/voltage, open circuit)

U0100.detailedDescription: A module that expects to talk to the engine PCM is no longer receiving messages from it.
commonCauses: Failed PCM (not powering on or not transmitting)
PCM power or ground circuit fault
CAN bus wiring break between PCM and other modules

U0101.detailedDescription: The Transmission Control Module is not responding on the bus.
commonCauses: CAN bus fault between TCM and PCM

U0121.shortDescription: Lost Communication With ABS Control Module
U0121.detailedDescription: The ABS/EBCM is not communicating on the bus.

U0155.shortDescription: Lost Communication With Instrument Panel Cluster
U0155.detailedDescription: Modules expecting to talk to the IPC are not receiving its messages.

U0401.detailedDescription: A module is receiving messages from the PCM but the data is implausible or out of range.
